segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with the sound of liquid pouring and glasses clinking, accompanied by a rhythmic, slightly ominous background music, as a hand places a dark bottle on a wooden bar. This transitions to a historical painting depicting people in period clothing drinking from mugs, visually linking alcohol to a long-standing cultural tradition. The scene then shifts to a modern bar setting where a man in a blue shirt gestures toward a small glass of beer, while a male voice asks, 'Is this amount of alcohol dangerous?' The narrative transitions to a split-screen interview where Adam, a man with curly hair and glasses in a plaid shirt, speaks into a silver microphone, stating in a clear, informative male voice, 'It's a serious question. In 2023, the World Health Organization issued this guidance that said, 'No level of alcohol consumption is safe for our health.'' This is immediately followed by a visual of the World Health Organization news release with the bold text. Joss, a woman with dark hair in a tie-dye top, then responds into her black microphone with a slightly higher-pitched, emphatic female voice, 'That's a pretty strong statement.' Adam echoes, 'Pretty strong statement,' before a narrator's male voice announces, 'And just last week, the US Surgeon General called for warning labels on drinks,' as a New York Times headline about alcohol labels warning of cancer risk is displayed. The scene changes to a TV in a living room showing a 'Today Show Australia' anchor, a woman with blonde hair in a maroon top, who reports in a clear female voice, 'Meanwhile, Australia has revised their drinking guidelines down. A sobering warning just in time for the silly season. Adults should have a maximum of 10 standard drinks a week.' The video then cuts to a vintage TV in a rustic room displaying Canadian alcohol guidelines from 2023, with graphics showing 'No more than 2 drinks a week,' as the narrator states, 'And Canada went even further.' A female voice, identified as a CBC News Anchor, adds, 'New recommended guidelines for Canadians advise no more than two drinks a week.' Street interviews follow, with CHCH TV Interviewee 1, a man with glasses and a plaid shirt, reacting with surprise in a male voice, 'Two drinks a week!' Another male voice dismissively adds, 'Well, that's just not uh feasible, not in this country.' A woman with dark hair in a black jacket, CHCH TV Interviewee 2, is seen as a third male voice reflects, 'Seems like uh there's different study out every couple years.' Adam then returns, explaining, 'For a long time, there was this guidance out there that a little bit of alcohol could actually be helpful for your health.' Joss recalls, 'Yeah, I remember seeing that, especially about red wine,' before the scene shifts to a FOX News Anchor, a woman with long dark hair, who enthusiastically reports, 'A glass of red wine is just as good as spending an hour at the gym,' with a graphic reinforcing this claim.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a split-screen interview featuring Adam, a man with curly hair and glasses in a blue plaid shirt, speaking into a silver microphone, questioning why there's contradictory information about moderate drinking and announcing that 'Howtown' will investigate decades of alcohol research. Joss, a woman with dark hair and bangs in a blue and white tie-dye top, is also visible in the split-screen, speaking into a black microphone. The scene then transitions to a dimly lit bar where a man with curly hair and glasses, wearing a dark blue button-up shirt, sits at a wooden table, holding a newspaper and gesturing as he speaks in a deep, resonant voice, accompanied by subtle background music with a plucked string instrument and a ticking clock sound. A close-up of the newspaper reveals the title 'Alcohol and Mortality' and a study from the 'Kaiser Permanente Experience.' The Bar Speaker's voice explains that in 1991, '60 Minutes' questioned why French people don't have more heart attacks despite eating much cheese. Archival male and female voices then suggest the answer lies in an 'inviting glass' and that cheese should be eaten with wine, which the Bar Speaker affirms as 'the perfect pairing.' A 3D animated data visualization appears, displaying bottle caps arranged by daily alcohol consumption categories (0, ≤2, 3-5, 6+ drinks/day), with colored spheres (gray for deceased, yellow for alive) indicating mortality rates, highlighting the 3-5 drinks/day group's highest survival rate and the 6+ group's highest mortality, all with an average age of 44.7. The Bar Speaker's voice explains this data comes from a 1981 study of 8,000 people from the San Francisco Bay Area, grouped by their drinking habits, emphasizing that each group had the same number of women and men. The segment concludes with the Bar Speaker continuing to speak while holding the newspaper, reinforcing the theme of healthy moderation in alcohol consumption.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a 3D animated visualization titled 'number of drinks/day,' displaying four groups of bottle caps on colored platforms (black, light blue, purple, red) corresponding to consumption levels (0, ≤2, 3-5, and 6+ drinks per day). A male narrator, later identified as Adam, explains the study's methodology, aiming to make the groups as similar as possible to associate health differences with drinking habits. As skulls appear beneath the 3-5 and 6+ groups, displaying mortality percentages (9.3% and 12.7%), Adam describes how they "watched and waited to see who got sick and who died." He reveals that after 10 years, the 6+ group saw the most deaths, followed by the 3-5 group, noting this is "not at all surprising" as "binge drinking and heavy drinking are really bad for you." The scene transitions to text defining 'binge drinking' (4+ for women, 5+ for men) and 'heavy drinking' (8+ for women, 15+ for men), with updated mortality rates (8.8%, 6.3%, 9.3%, and 12.7%). Adam then highlights a key finding: "the people who drank one to two drinks per day actually saw less death than the people who didn't drink at all." Bar graphs appear for 'accidents,' 'liver disease,' and 'cancer,' showing the highest deaths in the 6+ group, with annotations emphasizing 'highest for heavy drinkers' and 'lowest for moderate drinkers.' Adam elaborates that deaths from these causes were highest for heavy drinkers and lowest for moderate drinkers. A new bar graph for 'cardiac events' then shows the highest deaths in the 0 group, labeled 'highest for non-drinkers,' as Adam states that for heart problems, "non-drinkers actually had the worst outcomes." This is followed by a scatter plot of mortality rate vs. drinks/day, highlighting a U-shaped curve with the lowest mortality at ≤2 drinks/day. Adam describes this as a "sweet spot" where one avoids the "cancer and and disease of a heavy drinker" while "getting the protection for of your heart from being a moderate drinker." The scene cuts to a split-screen interview with Adam, a man with curly hair and glasses in a blue plaid shirt, speaking into a silver microphone, and Joss, a woman with dark hair and bangs in a tie-dye top, also speaking into a microphone. The video ends with a close-up of a scientific paper titled 'Gaziano et al. Alcohol and Mortality,' displaying U-shaped curves for 'Total Mortality' and 'CVD Mortality.' Adam concludes by noting, "That was just one study, but the pattern was seen over and over." Throughout the segment, a subtle, slightly ominous background music plays.

segments.integrated_caption: The video begins with a close-up of a scientific paper titled 'Di Castelnuovo et al. Wine and Beer Intake and Vascular Risk,' displaying a graph of relative risk versus wine intake, suggesting a J-shaped curve. A male voice, identified as Adam, explains that combining results from 34 studies in a meta-analysis yields "this graph with this so-called J-curve," as the visual transitions to a stylized animated J-curve graph on a blue background, labeled 'the J-curve,' showing relative risk of death decreasing with low alcohol intake before rising. Adam continues, clarifying that the graph shows "the number of drinks per day at the bottom, plotted against the relative risk of death." The scene then shifts to a split-screen interview where Joss, a woman with dark hair and bangs in a blue and white tie-dye top, speaking into a black microphone, explains that the J-curve "would suggest that your best case for your health or your risk of not dying is is is not to abstain completely, but to drink a very little bit." Adam, a man with curly hair and glasses in a blue plaid shirt, speaking into a silver microphone, confirms, "Exactly. A little is better than none is sort of the the takeaway." The video then shows a graph of 'US wine consumption' in gallons per person from 1980 to 2020, with a '60 Minutes' logo appearing over the dip in the 1990s, as Adam narrates that "Wine consumption in the US had been falling in the 80s, but then right around the time of all this coverage, the trend reversed." This is followed by a vintage TV screen displaying the text '... alcohol reduces the risk of heart disease ...' over grainy footage, while a deep, authoritative male voice from the vintage clip asserts, "There has been for years the belief by doctors that alcohol reduces the risk of heart disease. Now it's been all but confirmed." The next segment features Tanya Chikritzhs, a woman with short dark hair and glasses, wearing a red cardigan over a white collared shirt, speaking from a room with a blue abstract painting behind her. She appears in a split-screen with Adam, who is now wearing a maroon shirt. Tanya, speaking with a distinct Australian accent, explains, "It was just assumed to be so. It was a reality, it was a fact. Nobody questioned it, and if you did question it, you're pilloried." The video then shows a close-up of a scientific paper titled 'Error in Prospective Studies' by Tanya Chikritzhs, Kaye Fillmore, and William Kerr, as Adam narrates, "In the early 2000s, Tanya Chikritzhs was one of the scientists pointing out some issues with the J-curve." A subtle, almost metallic, background sound accompanies this segment. The final frames include a painting of a man in historical attire holding a wine glass, with the text '#1 - issues with the J-curve' and a number '2' overlay, followed by Tanya Chikritzhs speaking again, gesturing with her hand, and continuing her explanation: "Most of these observational studies misclassify people in terms of..."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a scientific paper discussing alcohol consumption, highlighting questions about drinking habits and the number of drinks per day. A female voice, belonging to Tanya Chikritzhs, asks, "How do they define a non-drinker?" as the scene transitions to her speaking from a room with a blue abstract painting behind her, wearing a red cardigan over a white collared shirt and gesturing. The video then cuts to a split-screen, showing Tanya on the right and Adam on the left. Adam, a man with curly hair and glasses, wearing a maroon shirt, speaks into a silver microphone from a room with shelves holding a green rotary phone, keyboard, and globe, explaining that some studies ask about alcohol consumption in the past year, week, or three days. Tanya then continues, emphasizing with hand gestures, that many who claim to be non-drinkers were actually drinkers in their 20s and 30s, drawing a parallel to how tobacco studies would never call a former heavy smoker a non-smoker, yet "we do it with alcohol research all the time." A dark, painted image of a skull smoking a cigarette briefly appears, symbolizing the dangers of substance use. Returning to the split-screen, Tanya explains that "ex-drinkers tend to be unhealthier." Adam elaborates, stating there's "a big group of people who used to drink a lot, then their health started deteriorating, so they stopped." He adds that even actual lifetime abstainers may have avoided drinking due to existing health conditions, and "lumping all these people into the non-drinkers column has the potential to push up the death rate of that whole group." A 3D data visualization then appears, showing bottle caps arranged by daily alcohol consumption categories (0, ≤2, 3-5, 6+ drinks/day), with green caps indicating 'former drinkers' and 'sick abstainers' within the '0' category, and colored spheres showing mortality rates with an average age of 44.7. This is followed by a J-curve graph illustrating mortality rates increasing with higher alcohol consumption, with a notable spike at 0 drinks/day attributed to sick abstainers. Adam concludes, "And that's just the first issue." The segment ends with a historical painting of a man holding a wine glass, overlaid with text reading '2 issues with the J-curve #2 - rich drinkers,' hinting at further discussion on socioeconomic factors.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a split-screen interview featuring Adam, a man with curly hair and glasses wearing a plaid shirt, speaking into a silver microphone, asking Joss, a woman with dark hair and bangs in a blue and white tie-dye top, if she saw a TikTok about 'Hank's Razor,' to which she responds with an 'Mm-mm.' Adam's room has shelves displaying a green rotary phone, keyboard, and globe, while Joss is in a room with a plant and orange couch. The scene transitions to a close-up of a smartphone on a wooden table, displaying a video of a man in a gray t-shirt and cap, who, with subtitles, humorously requests that if he dies soon, his concept for explaining phenomena by socioeconomic status be called 'Hank's Razor,' emphasizing that it's 'probably that rather than the thing that you're measuring,' before reassuring he's 'not gonna die anytime soon.' A glass of tea is visible next to the phone. The video then returns to the split-screen with Adam and Joss, both smiling and engaged, as Adam expresses hope for the man's longevity but states his intention to apply 'Hank's Razor' to studies, specifically asking if drinking levels are connected to wealth, prompting an appreciative 'Love it' from Joss. Adam then appears alone, holding a printed document, as a scientific paper titled 'Lifetime income patterns and alcohol consumption' is displayed, with Adam's voice confirming that drinking can indeed be connected to wealth, highlighting the finding: 'Lower income was associated with higher odds of abstinence and of heavy drinking, relative to light/moderate drinking.' This is followed by a 3D data visualization showing bottle caps arranged by daily alcohol consumption (0, ≤2, 3-5, 6+ drinks/day), with dollar signs appearing on some caps to indicate socioeconomic status, as Adam explains that the moderate drinking group likely contains a disproportionate number of rich people, who benefit from factors like 'more time for exercise, access...' The segment concludes with a historical painting of a man in 17th-century attire holding a wine glass, overlaid with the text '#1 - issues with the J-curve' and a number '2', suggesting a thematic connection to alcohol consumption patterns and societal factors.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a richly detailed historical painting of a lavish banquet, setting a tone of indulgence, as a male voice, identified as the Bar Speaker, explains how moderate drinkers often have access to better doctors, medicine, food, and less pollution, leading to a lower death rate due to higher educational attainment and healthier lifestyles. This visual context transitions to a close-up of a scientific paper discussing alcohol consumption and mortality, followed by a simple scatter plot showing mortality rates against daily drink consumption, with an arrow pointing to the lowest rate at ≤2 drinks/day. The Bar Speaker, a man with curly hair and glasses in a dark blue shirt, is then seen in a dimly lit bar, gesturing as he speaks directly to the camera, with a small glass of amber liquid appearing on his wooden table. He questions if studies are truly showing the benefits of moderate drinking or merely the benefits of being rich, asking if the J-curve, which is then overlaid as a stylized animated graph illustrating the relative risk of death versus drinks per day, is 'just a mirage.' The video then cuts to a sponsorship screen for 'GROUND News,' which the Bar Speaker introduces as today's sponsor, describing it as a website and app that gathers news headlines from 50,000 sources, categorizing them by political bias, factual accuracy, and ownership. This is shown through a smartphone interface displaying various news articles and a detailed bias and factuality analysis tool. The scene transitions to a split-screen video call where the Bar Speaker, now in a plaid shirt, speaks into a silver microphone, while on the left, a Woman in Video Call with dark hair and earphones speaks into a black microphone. She adds that Ground News allows users to browse headlines and get additional metadata about the source. The video concludes with the Bar Speaker highlighting a 'Blindspot' feature on the 'GROUND News' website interface, which shows news stories with minimal coverage from one political side, accompanied by bar graphs indicating media bias distribution, before his audio cuts off.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dark news interface displaying headlines like 'Senior DOJ officials leaked investigation details before election,' which a male voice (Adam) notes was "much more heavily covered on the right," and 'World’s 500 Richest Surpassed $10 Trillion in Wealth,' which he points out was covered more on the left, illustrating how news is "covered way more by one side or the other." Adam, seen in a split-screen video call gesturing while speaking into a silver microphone, explains to an attentive woman with dark hair and bangs that "we're in our own silos of news," making it easy to "lose track of what other people are experiencing." His explanation transitions to a promotional screen displaying a QR code and the URL 'ground.news/howtown,' offering "40% OFF Vantage Plan" for the service he's promoting. A distinct sound effect signals a shift as the scene moves to a dimly lit bar where the same man, now identified as the Bar Speaker, sits with a beer, gesturing as his voice announces, "Now back to booze and interrogating that famous J-curve." A close-up of a scientific paper titled 'Association Between Daily Alcohol Intake and Risk of All-Cause Mortality' is shown, followed by an animated J-curve graph illustrating the risk of death versus number of drinks per day. The Bar Speaker, gesturing, explains that a 2023 research team found the "familiar pattern" in 107 papers, but after adjusting for lifestyle differences, the "benefit of moderate drinking shrank," becoming "no longer statistically significant," leaving open whether it was a "slight cardiovascular benefit" or "confounding factors."

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with Tanya Chikritzhs, a woman with short dark hair and glasses in a red cardigan, speaking directly to the camera, gesturing as she discusses the difficulty of eliminating confounding in observational studies. Her voice is clear and assertive. The scene then transitions to a close-up of a scientific paper titled 'Alcohol use and burden for 195 countries and territories,' while a male voice (the Bar Speaker) provides a voiceover, explaining this "giant meta-analysis" that gathered data from 195 countries and 28 million people. A world map then illustrates current drinker prevalence by region, followed by animated graphs on a blue background. The Bar Speaker's voice continues, detailing a J-shaped curve for ischaemic heart disease risk versus drinks per day, but emphasizes that any slight heart benefit is "more than canceled out by an increased risk of cancer," concluding there's "no net benefit for moderate drinking." Tanya Chikritzhs reappears, questioning, "why on earth would you recommend somebody to drink for heart health? Makes no sense at all." The Bar Speaker then appears on screen in a dimly lit bar, speaking directly to the camera about how most science discussed so far comes from "observational studies that look for associations." The video cuts to a split-screen interview where Adam, in a plaid shirt, begins to explain that to "prove causation, the ideal setup is..." before Joss, in a tie-dye top, interjects to complete his thought: "A randomized controlled trial." The segment concludes with an animated visualization of a randomized controlled trial, showing bottle caps being assigned to 'drink' or 'don’t drink' groups, as the Bar Speaker's voice explains that in such a trial, the amount subjects drink is "assigned by the researcher, chosen by the flip of a coin."
segments.visual_original_caption: The video begins with Tanya Chikritzhs, a woman with short dark hair and glasses, wearing a red cardigan over a white collared shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a room with a blue abstract painting behind her. She gestures with her hands as she discusses a scientific paper, which is then shown in close-up: a study titled 'Alcohol use and burden for 195 countries and territories, 1990–2016: a systematic analysis for the Global Burden of Disease Study 2016.' The video transitions to a world map illustrating current drinker prevalence by region, followed by a series of animated graphs on a blue background. These graphs depict the risk of death from ischaemic heart disease, cirrhosis, and all attributable health outcomes in relation to the number of drinks per day, with a J-shaped curve for heart disease and a steadily increasing risk for other outcomes. The scene shifts to a man with curly hair and glasses, the Bar Speaker, sitting at a wooden table in a dimly lit bar with a small glass of beer, speaking to the camera. The video then cuts to a split-screen interview with Adam, wearing a plaid shirt and speaking into a silver microphone, and Joss, wearing a blue and white tie-dye top and speaking into a black microphone, both in their respective home settings. The segment concludes with an animated visualization of a randomized controlled trial, showing bottle caps being assigned to 'drink' or 'don’t drink' groups by a researcher’s hand, symbolizing the experimental design.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with an animated visualization of a randomized controlled trial, showing a researcher assigning bottle caps to 'drink' and 'don't drink' groups, as Adam's clear male voice explains the process of comparing "two truly random groups." The scene then transitions to a split-screen video call where Joss, a woman with dark hair and bangs in a blue and white tie-dye top, asks, "Can you randomly select people to drink five drinks a day for a long period of time?" Adam, a man with curly hair and glasses in a plaid shirt, responds, gesturing into his silver microphone, highlighting the "challenges of creating a randomized controlled trial for alcohol," including ethical concerns about potentially harming participants. He continues, detailing practical concerns like needing to follow participants for "decades" to observe long-term effects. The animation returns, showing the researcher pointing to a '5 drinks/day' card and a bottle cap with '60', visually reinforcing the discussion of consumption metrics. Adam's voice then introduces the "first big randomized controlled trial" in 2015, designed to last "six years with one group not drinking at all, and the other drinking about one drink a day," aiming for a sample size of "about 8,000 people." This is visually accompanied by a close-up of the scientific paper for 'The Moderate Alcohol and Cardiovascular Health Trial (MACH15),' which mentions 'one alcoholic drink daily over an average of six years.' The video alternates back to Adam and Joss, with Joss, listening intently and occasionally adjusting her earphones, reacting with surprise, "8,000 people for six years? Like that's a huge effort." Adam concludes by emphasizing that this scale is "huge" especially "for a randomized controlled trial," distinguishing it from observational studies.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video begins with an animated visualization of a randomized controlled trial, where a hand labeled 'researcher' assigns bottle caps to two groups: 'drink' (on a red card) and 'don’t drink' (on a black card), illustrating the design of a clinical study. The scene transitions to a split-screen video call featuring Adam, a man with curly hair and glasses in a plaid shirt speaking into a silver microphone, and Joss, a woman with dark hair and bangs in a blue and white tie-dye top, speaking into a black microphone. They appear to be discussing the trial, with Adam gesturing as he speaks. The animation returns, showing the 'researcher' hand pointing to a red card labeled '5 drinks/day' and then to a single bottle cap with the number '60' appearing beside it, symbolizing a daily consumption metric. The video then cuts to a close-up of a scientific paper titled 'The Moderate Alcohol and Cardiovascular Health Trial (MACH15): Design and methods for a randomized trial of moderate alcohol consumption and cardiometabolic risk,' with text highlighting 'one alcoholic drink daily over an average of six years.' The scene alternates back to Adam and Joss, who continue their discussion, with Joss listening intently and occasionally adjusting her earphones. The overall tone is informative, blending animated data visualization with a live conversation between two experts.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a split-screen interview featuring Adam, a man with curly hair and glasses in a plaid shirt, speaking into a silver microphone, and Joss, a woman with dark hair and bangs in a blue and white tie-dye top, speaking into a black microphone. Adam's clear, explanatory male voice immediately begins discussing how the New York Times 'blew the lid off' a study. The scene transitions to a close-up of the 'New York Times Article' with the headline 'Federal Agency Courted Alcohol Industry to Fund Study on Benefits of Moderate Drinking,' as Adam's voice details how alcohol companies funded a $100 million NIH study and scientists suggested it would endorse moderate drinking as healthy. He then states the study was canceled. The video cuts back to Adam and Joss, where Joss's conversational female voice interjects with a dismayed 'Oh' and 'Bummer.' Adam's voice continues, explaining that while a randomized controlled trial was 'dashed,' they're 'not out of tools yet,' suggesting genetics as an alternative. The scene then shows the Bar Speaker, who is Adam in a dark blue shirt, sitting at a wooden table in a dimly lit bar with a small glass of beer, gesturing as he introduces 'Mendelian randomization' as a new research method. An animated graphic of two hands with a thumbs-down gesture appears, followed by a historical painting overlaid with '4' and the text 'Mendelian randomization.' Joss's voice then asks, 'This is the guy with the peas, right?' as a portrait of Gregor Mendel appears. Adam's voice confirms, identifying him as 'Friar Gregor Mendel' who worked out the basics of genetics by 'obsessively studying pea plants,' finding that 'genes are randomly distributed among offspring.' An animated sequence of purple and white flowers symbolizes genetic variation before returning to Adam in the bar setting, continuing his explanation of the first step of a Mendelian randomization study.

The scene transitions to a close-up of a New York Times article titled 'Federal Agency Courted Alcohol Industry to Fund Study on Benefits of Moderate Drinking,' highlighting text about Anheuser Busch InBev, Heineken, and other alcohol companies funding a $100 million NIH study through donations to a private foundation. The article also mentions that scientists gave talks suggesting the study would endorse moderate drinking as healthy. The video then cuts back to Adam and Joss, followed by a scene of the Bar Speaker, a man with curly hair and glasses in a dark blue shirt, sitting at a wooden table in a dimly lit bar with a small glass of beer, gesturing as he speaks. An animated sequence of purple and white flowers appears, symbolizing genetic variation, before returning to the Bar Speaker in the bar setting, continuing his explanation.

segments.integrated_caption: The video begins with Kiran Biddinger from Princeton University, seen speaking directly to the camera in a well-lit room with a bookshelf, explaining how genes can predispose individuals to 'drink more.' The scene transitions to a split-screen interview where Adam, in a room with eclectic shelves, is heard discussing 'the flushing gene' that Joss has previously covered. This is followed by a cut to a YouTube interface displaying a Vox video titled 'Asian flush, explained,' featuring a diagram of alcohol metabolism. The video then returns to Adam and Joss, with Joss gesturing as she speaks, confirming 'The one that I have?' and explaining that 'it codes for an enzyme that metabolizes alcohol.' Adam then clarifies that her version of the gene means 'less of this enzyme.' Joss continues, describing how 'this toxic substance builds up in our bodies and makes us turn fuchsia in my case, like so red. It's very uncomfortable. I get a terrible headache,' explaining why she 'never picked up drinking,' and noting her sister 'doesn't have it.' Adam then describes this as 'a perfect example of like flipping a coin, these two daughters of the same family, one has it, one doesn't.' The video then displays a world map with color gradients indicating the prevalence of a genetic trait, with darker blue regions concentrated in East Asia, while Kiran Biddinger's voice explains that 'globally, the drink less version of a gene might be more common in some populations,' but within a specific subpopulation, 'your chance of carrying that variant should be random, not influenced by your social class and certainly not your lifestyle.' This is followed by an animated graphic of bottle caps, some turning red and purple to represent 'drink less' and 'drink more' categories. The final scene shows the Bar Speaker, a man with curly hair and glasses, in a dimly lit bar, gesturing while speaking at a wooden table with a small glass of beer, stating that 'That doesn't mean that people with this drink more variant are destined to become heavy drinkers or even drinkers at all. It just means that...' before the audio cuts off.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with the Bar Speaker, a man with curly hair and glasses in a dark blue shirt, gesturing at a dimly lit wooden bar table as he explains that people with a specific gene version tend to drink more. The scene transitions to a split-screen, where Adam, with curly hair and glasses in a plaid shirt, speaks into a silver microphone, likening these genetic predispositions to "random tickets" making individuals "more likely to drink, say, an extra glass a day." Kiran Biddinger, a man with dark hair in a dark t-shirt, is also seen speaking in front of a bookshelf, describing Mendelian randomization as "nature's clinical trial." Kiran continues, detailing a 2022 Mendelian randomization study he and his colleagues published, involving 370,000 people in the UK with European ancestry. An animated 3D Data Visualization then shows bottle caps being sorted by a green 'genetics' hand into 'drink more' and 'drink less' categories, followed by a close-up of a Scientific Paper Visualization from JAMA Network Open, highlighting a study of 'n=371 463' and 'genetic ancestry.' Kiran's voice explains that within this group, drinking more was associated with negative outcomes. He reappears, elaborating that people with "drink more" gene variants had worse heart health across various measures, visually supported by a graph showing 'worse' and 'better' outcomes for hypertension, coronary artery disease, heart attack, and stroke, with red dots for 'drink more' and black dots for 'drink less.' The graph then transitions to a logarithmic scale, illustrating an increasing odds ratio for coronary artery disease with more drinks per day, as Kiran concludes that increased alcohol intake appears to cause a higher risk of cardiovascular disease, regardless of one's position on the alcohol consumption spectrum. The scene shifts to a split-screen, with Adam on the left speaking into a silver microphone, expressing significant doubt about a previously believed protective effect of alcohol. On the right, Joss, a woman with dark hair and bangs in a blue and white tie-dye top, speaks into a black microphone, suggesting that at the very least, the prior consensus that "drinking a little bit is better" can be questioned.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a glass of beer, its golden liquid, rising bubbles, and condensation setting a thematic tone, as a voice concludes a thought with "nothing." The scene transitions to a split-screen video call where Adam, a man with curly hair and glasses in a plaid shirt, speaking into a silver microphone against a backdrop of eclectic shelves, asks Kiran Biddinger, a man with dark hair in a black t-shirt in front of a bookshelf, if he's "recently of drinking age," to which Kiran replies "Yes." Adam continues, noting Kiran is "in the midst of this research but you're also in college." An upbeat, slightly distorted, celebratory music track with a "Hey!" vocal interjects before Adam asks, "How do you think about this?" The video cuts to a full-screen view of Kiran, who, speaking with expressive hand movements, states, "As someone who's recently come of drinking age, I think over the course of a lifetime, cutting back on your alcohol intake is healthy," adding, "But having a drink every now and then also isn't gonna kill you. There's harms to a to a lot of stuff that we do." The scene shifts back to the split-screen, now including Joss, a woman with dark hair and bangs in a blue and white tie-dye top, visible speaking into a black microphone. Adam adds, "There's tons of things that if you didn't do them, you'd be safer," to which Joss interjects with a lighthearted tone, "Like go outside." Adam agrees, "Yeah, go outside," and Joss adds, "Leave your house," followed by Adam's observation, "Drive for a lot of people." Kiran then points out, "People die in car crashes, it doesn't mean that we're gonna not drive our cars." Adam poses the question, "So what's the best way to put the risk of drinking in perspective?" The video then cuts to a dimly lit bar where the Bar Speaker, a man with curly hair and glasses in a dark blue shirt, sits at a wooden table with a small glass of alcohol and a document, gesturing as he speaks. He suggests, "Let's go back to that giant global meta-study that looked at a bunch of different diseases that can be caused by alcohol." The camera zooms in on the document, revealing a scientific paper titled 'Alcohol use and burden for 195 countries and territories, 1990–2016: a systematic analysis for the Global Burden of Disease Study 2016.' Close-ups of the document's text highlight health risks such as 'Esophageal cancer,' 'Ischaemic stroke,' and 'Tuberculosis,' as the Bar Speaker elaborates, "In high-income countries, the most common one is cancer. In low-income countries, it's tuberculosis because alcohol hurts your immune system. And there are 21 other diseases on the list." He continues, "Of course, you can get those diseases even if you don't drink. The authors found that out of a population of a..." The segment ends with a black screen featuring a single bottle cap, followed by a grid of white dots forming a pattern, suggesting a data visualization or transition, as the Bar Speaker's voice trails off.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a data visualization, described by a male narrator, illustrating the increasing health risks associated with alcohol consumption: starting with 100,000 non-drinkers where 914 develop alcohol-related diseases, then transitioning to 100,000 light drinkers (919 affected), moderate drinkers (979 affected), and finally heavy drinkers (1,251 affected), emphasizing the progressively rising risk. Joss, a woman with dark hair and bangs in a blue and white tie-dye top, then appears speaking into a black microphone, commenting in a calm voice, "That doesn't seem so bad." Adam, a man with curly hair and glasses in a plaid shirt, follows, speaking into a silver microphone, adding in a clear voice that while it "doesn't seem so bad," the absolute numbers increase over time. The video returns to a data visualization, a graph titled 'all attributable health outcomes' with a red curve rising from 0 to 14 drinks per day, as the narrator explains how risk rises faster with heavy drinking but stays low at two drinks a day. Joss reappears, observing that "It's not a J, but it's not that steep of a line either." The narrator then asks, "So just how dangerous is this?" The scene shifts to the Bar Speaker, a man with curly hair and glasses in a dark blue button-up shirt, sitting at a wooden table in a dimly lit bar with a glass of amber liquid, who thoughtfully discusses the "frustrating amount of uncertainty" and the growing agreement that even a little alcohol can increase the risk of cancer and other diseases.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with the Bar Speaker, a man with curly hair and glasses in a dark blue button-up shirt, seated at a wooden table in a dimly lit bar, explaining in a calm, informative male voice how moderate doses don't significantly increase risk and how recent announcements from the World Health Organization and the Surgeon General's warnings are part of a "course correction" against decades of headlines that led people to believe moderate drinking was medicinal. As he speaks, the scene transitions to a close-up of a frothy beer mug, followed by a series of newspaper clippings with headlines promoting moderate drinking benefits, including 'Alcohol Labels Should Warn of Cancer Risk' and 'Moderate drinking shows benefits,' alongside a black-and-white 'BOOZE BENEFITS' headline from June 12, 2000. The Bar Speaker continues, noting that public health officials aren't trying to make everyone stop drinking. The video then cuts to Tanya Chikritzhs, a woman with short dark hair and glasses in a red cardigan, speaking animatedly in a distinct female voice from a room with a blue abstract painting, humorously stating, "People assume I'm Professor No Fun Pants," and identifying herself as an "occasional drinker." The scene shifts to a split-screen interview featuring Adam, a man with curly hair and glasses in a plaid shirt, speaking into a silver microphone in a room with eclectic shelves, and Joss, a woman with dark hair and bangs, listening attentively into a black microphone in a room with a plant and orange couch. Adam, in a clear, informative male voice, emphasizes that "everyone knows that there is some risk," and that this risk is different for every individual, citing family history of cancer and alcoholism as factors that can push up one's baseline risk, and mentioning other impacts on sleep, mental health, decisions, and relationships. The video returns to the Bar Speaker in the bar, who discusses the "flip side" of drinking being an important part of social life or community. A graph titled 'all attributable health outcomes' then appears, showing a rising red line indicating increased risk with more drinks per day. The minute concludes with a somber painting of two men at a table, one slumped over, followed by a vibrant painting of a group toasting with champagne in a sunlit outdoor setting.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a split-screen interview featuring Joss, a woman with dark hair and bangs in a blue and white tie-dye top, speaking expressively into a black microphone in a bright room, and Adam, a man with curly hair and glasses in a plaid shirt, smiling and nodding into a silver microphone in a room with eclectic items. Joss, with a clear, articulate female voice, discusses the public's tendency to accept binary answers, stating, "It's either can't do it or all clear, you know, for anything." Adam, in a male voice, interjects with "Right, right." The scene transitions to a full-screen view of Joss, who continues to gesture and adjust her earphones, as Adam's voice expresses, "It's messy, it's difficult. I'm not sure how to finish this episode with in a satisfying way," prompting a light laugh and a comforting "It'll come to you" from Joss. Adam then says, "To your health," before the video cuts to him in a dimly lit bar, now wearing a dark blue button-up shirt, seated at a wooden table, sipping from a small glass and gesturing as a gentle acoustic guitar melody begins to play. On-screen text credits Adam Cole and Joss Fong as writers and producers. Adam's voice, now with a more formal, narrative tone over the music, invites viewers to join their Patreon for a "monthly Science Paper Book Club."
